food_subgroup: Mild Kerala coconut milk fish curry (white/yellow, no red chilli)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Fish Moilee. Nearest parent is a mild fish in coconut milk sauce. A new concept for Kerala mild coconut fish curry preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish in mild coconut milk sauce, Kerala style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Fish Moilee. A04FM is used as the closest defensible parent for fish in coconut milk sauce preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Fish Moilee as a specific prepared dish.

main_ingredients: firm white fish (pomfret, kingfish, or seer fish)

serving.description: 1 bowl (2 pieces of fish + coconut gravy)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Fish Moilee is approximately 250 g, comprising two pieces of firm white fish in coconut milk gravy, as typically served at a Kerala restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Moilee — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EX000025
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN entry EX000025 for Fish Moilee. Core macros and most micronutrients are verified at confidence 1.0; copper, manganese, and vitamin K are enriched from supplementary JSON at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish and Coconut Milk Preparations — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate fish curry category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Moilee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate fish and coconut milk curry category. Fish and coconut milk contribute negligible digestible carbohydrate; the primary carbohydrate sources are onion and trace starch from spices. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.4 g, confirming very low GL. This estimate is consistent with NIN India guidance and peer-reviewed literature on low-GI fish-based dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 285 mg per 100 g (713 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving of Fish Moilee contributes approximately 713 mg sodium, representing about 31% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and reduced added salt during preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (GI 30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish Moilee is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate load (glycemic load ~2.4 per serving), high protein content supporting satiety, and medium-chain triglycerides from coconut milk that do not acutely raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.8 g per 100 g (12.0 g per 250 g serving) primarily from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Fish Moilee provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from fish and vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100 g), the saturated fat content from coconut milk (4.8 g/100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol; serving with steamed rice rather than appam reduces overall energy density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from fish, curcumin from turmeric, and lauric acid from coconut milk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Moilee is a suitable choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, turmeric contributes curcumin with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100 g) supports immune function and bone health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Moilee (also known as Meen Moilee or Meen Molee) is one of the most delicate and mild fish curries in Indian cuisine, originating from the coastal region of Kerala in South India. The dish features firm white fish — typically pomfret, kingfish (seer fish), or tenggiri — that is lightly sautéed and then gently simmered in a pale-yellow to white coconut milk gravy. The cooking technique uses thin coconut milk first to poach the fish, followed by thick coconut milk added at the end to create a creamy, subtly rich sauce. The spice profile is intentionally restrained: turmeric provides the pale yellow colour, green chillies (not red chilli powder) add mild heat, fresh ginger and onion form the aromatic base, and curry leaves and coconut oil finish the dish. Unlike most Indian fish curries, Fish Moilee contains no tomato, no red chilli powder, and no tamarind, resulting in a clean, white or pale-yellow appearance. The flavour is mild, slightly sweet from the coconut milk, and showcases the natural sweetness of fresh fish. The dish is said to reflect Portuguese culinary influence on Kerala coastal cooking. It is traditionally served with appam (lacy rice-flour pancakes) or steamed rice. Nutritionally, Fish Moilee provides 108 kcal per 100 g, with 10.8 g protein, 6.5 g fat (4.8 g saturated from coconut milk), and only 3.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a very low glycemic index of approximately 30. It is a good source of selenium (22 mcg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), niacin (4.0 mg), vitamin D (3.5 mcg), and potassium (302 mg). The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely available at Kerala and South Indian restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: is fish moilee good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition), the primary approved source for Indian dishes, with the majority of values at confidence 1.0. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are at confidence 0.9 from enriched JSON. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with approved GI database methodology.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g as confirmed by ICMR-NIN source data; this is expected for a traditionally prepared fish and coconut milk curry with no hydrogenated fats. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported in the source; set to -1. Water content not reported in source; set to -1. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate fish curry category; no direct GI measurement for Fish Moilee exists in the database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (30 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.4. FoodEx2 code A04FM is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact code exists for Fish Moilee. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists for this dish. Portions list in input contained duplicate entries; deduplicated to two distinct portions in output. The dish is not a Singaporean dish by origin; multilingual requirements follow the Indian/South Asian cuisine category. Hindi and Tamil names are included as the dish originates from South India and is widely consumed in the Indian diaspora in Singapore.
